description: Sound reaches a person or animal through 'shonui', the external and internal organ of hearing. 'hea' (hear) reports auditory reception, while 'sheluo' (listen) adds directed attention. Hearing a sound does not by itself claim that anyone listened or understood it.
articulatory_notes: Raise the tongue blade toward the postalveolar region and send unvoiced air through its central groove for 'sh', then round the lips around middle-height back 'o'. Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and release into stressed high-back rounded 'u'. Raise the tongue into high-front unrounded 'i' for the separate final syllable.
sound_symbolism: The quiet postalveolar hiss at the opening gives way to a resonant nasal and two high vowels. The form moves from soft outside air toward a more inward sound, which can help a learner remember the receiving organ without treating any syllable as a definition.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Separating an ear, hearing, and deliberate listening prevents a speaker from treating physical reception as proof of attention or agreement.

semantic_domains.communication: the bodily organ that receives speech and other sound
semantic_domains.physical: the external and internal organ of hearing
